content.text: An SLA breach is not simply a late ticket — it is a contractual failure that carries commercial consequences for enterprise customers and signals a capacity or process problem that must be investigated and remediated. The breach escalation process defines what happens when an SLA clock is about to expire or has already expired: who gets alerted, what automatic actions occur, how the customer is communicated with, and how the account relationship is protected. A well-designed breach process catches most at-risk tickets before they breach, handles the ones that do breach with a consistent response, and ensures every breach is reviewed for root cause.

attrs.alt: SLA breach escalation flow showing system alerts, support agent actions, management notifications, customer communication, and account team involvement across five swimlanes timed at T=0, T=80%, T=100% breach, and T=150%

content.text: Breach detection uses a two-stage alert model: a warning at 80% of the SLA window elapsed with no first response, and a hard alert at 100%. The 80% warning gives the assigned agent time to respond before the breach occurs. The 100% hard alert fires when the breach has already happened and triggers automatic ownership reassignment. Waiting until 100% to fire any alert means every breach has already occurred before the process begins — the warning stage is what converts breach detection from retrospective to preventive.

The notify_targets method returns a list of channels and individuals to alert at each stage. The logic is layered: the more severe the stage, the wider the notification fan-out. For enterprise accounts, the account executive is looped in at the breach stage because they manage the commercial relationship and need to be prepared for a customer conversation about the credit. For non-enterprise accounts, the AE is not included — the support manager handles any follow-up directly.

When a ticket breaches its first-response SLA, the automatic response is reassignment to the support manager's backup queue — not deletion of the original assignment, but transfer of ownership to a queue that a senior agent monitors. The original agent is notified so they know the ticket has been escalated. This matters because the most common cause of first-response SLA breaches is an agent who was assigned the ticket but was pulled into an incident or went offline. Automatic reassignment prevents the ticket from sitting unnoticed while the clock continues.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class TicketReassignment:
    ticket_id: str
    from_agent: str
    to_queue: str                  # backup queue, not a specific agent
    reason: str
    reassigned_at: datetime
    original_agent_notified: bool = False
    manager_notified: bool = False

def breach_reassign(
    ticket_id: str,
    current_agent: str,
    priority: str,
    is_enterprise: bool,
) -> TicketReassignment:
    # P0/P1 enterprise: goes to dedicated enterprise backup queue
    # P0/P1 standard: goes to L2 senior queue
    # P2/P3: goes to general backup queue
    if is_enterprise and priority in ("P0", "P1"):
        queue = "enterprise-priority-backup"
    elif priority in ("P0", "P1"):
        queue = "l2-senior-backup"
    else:
        queue = "general-backup"

    reassignment = TicketReassignment(
        ticket_id=ticket_id,
        from_agent=current_agent,
        to_queue=queue,
        reason=f"First-response SLA breached for {priority} ticket",
        reassigned_at=datetime.utcnow(),
    )
    # Notifications are sent as side effects by the caller
    return reassignment


def send_reassignment_notifications(r: TicketReassignment) -> None:
    # These are illustrative; real implementation uses a notification service
    _post_dm(r.from_agent, f"Ticket {r.ticket_id} has been reassigned due to SLA breach. No action needed from you — the backup queue will handle it.")
    _post_channel("#support-management", f"SLA breach reassignment: {r.ticket_id} -> {r.to_queue} (was @{r.from_agent})")


def _post_dm(agent: str, msg: str) -> None: ...
def _post_channel(channel: str, msg: str) -> None: ...

The queue-based reassignment model (rather than direct-to-agent) is intentional. Assigning directly to another agent risks creating a second idle ticket if that agent is also unavailable. A monitored queue guarantees a human is actively watching it. The manager who owns the backup queue is responsible for claiming each reassigned ticket within 15 minutes — their own SLA for backup queue claims is tracked as a management metric.

Communicating with the customer when an SLA is breached requires balancing two competing concerns: the customer deserves honesty about the delay, but a premature or poorly worded message can escalate a dissatisfied customer into a churned one. The breach communication framework provides templates for different breach scenarios and specifies the maximum elapsed time before a customer message must be sent. For P0 enterprise tickets, that maximum is 30 minutes after the breach is detected.
Acknowledgment + investigation status + ETA for next update
Status update + revised ETA + workaround if available

The message must not promise a specific resolution time unless engineering has confirmed one — overpromising a resolution window and then missing it creates a second breach in the customer's perception, even if the SLA clock no longer applies to the follow-up. A safer pattern is to commit to an update interval ("we'll send an update every 2 hours") rather than a resolution time ("this will be fixed by 3 PM").

The full breach escalation process is a state machine with well-defined transitions. A ticket starts in the PENDING state when no first response has been sent. It moves to WARNING at 80%, BREACHED at 100%, and EXTENDED at 150% — but it can also move to RESOLVED at any point if a first response is sent. The RESOLVED state terminates the first-response escalation path; a separate state machine governs resolution SLA tracking.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from enum import Enum

class BreachState(str, Enum):
    PENDING  = "pending"   # no first response, SLA still running
    WARNING  = "warning"   # > 80% elapsed, agent notified
    BREACHED = "breached"  # > 100% elapsed, reassigned
    EXTENDED = "extended"  # > 150% elapsed, VP + AE looped in
    RESOLVED = "resolved"  # first response sent; breach tracking ends

# State transition table: (current_state, event) -> next_state
TRANSITIONS: dict[tuple[BreachState, str], BreachState] = {
    (BreachState.PENDING, "pct_80"):        BreachState.WARNING,
    (BreachState.PENDING, "pct_100"):       BreachState.BREACHED,   # skipped 80% somehow
    (BreachState.PENDING, "first_response"):BreachState.RESOLVED,
    (BreachState.WARNING, "pct_100"):       BreachState.BREACHED,
    (BreachState.WARNING, "first_response"):BreachState.RESOLVED,
    (BreachState.BREACHED, "pct_150"):      BreachState.EXTENDED,
    (BreachState.BREACHED, "first_response"):BreachState.RESOLVED,
    (BreachState.EXTENDED, "first_response"):BreachState.RESOLVED,
}

@dataclass
class BreachStateMachine:
    ticket_id: str
    state: BreachState = BreachState.PENDING
    history: list[tuple[str, BreachState]] = field(default_factory=list)

    def transition(self, event: str) -> BreachState:
        next_state = TRANSITIONS.get((self.state, event))
        if next_state is None:
            return self.state   # no-op: transition not defined
        self.history.append((event, self.state))
        self.state = next_state
        return self.state

    def is_terminal(self) -> bool:
        return self.state == BreachState.RESOLVED

    def highest_breach_state(self) -> BreachState | None:
        breach_order = [BreachState.WARNING, BreachState.BREACHED, BreachState.EXTENDED]
        for state in reversed(breach_order):
            if any(s == state for _, s in self.history):
                return state
        return None

The highest_breach_state method is used in post-breach review to understand how far the breach progressed before resolution. A ticket that resolved from the EXTENDED state is treated differently in the post-breach process than one that resolved from the BREACHED state — the former requires a management review and almost always triggers a service credit, while the latter may be handled at the support manager level if the root cause is clear.

Every P0 breach and every P1 breach for an enterprise customer requires a post-breach review. The review is not a blame exercise — it is a structured investigation of why the SLA was missed so the process can be improved. The review is completed within 24 hours of the breach being resolved, while the details are still fresh. It follows a fixed template to ensure consistency and comparability across reviews.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, date
from enum import Enum

class BreachRootCause(str, Enum):
    STAFFING_GAP        = "staffing_gap"        # not enough agents on shift
    MISSED_NOTIFICATION = "missed_notification" # alert fired but went unnoticed
    MISCLASSIFIED_TIER  = "misclassified_tier"  # P0 incorrectly classified as P2
    COMPLEX_HANDOFF     = "complex_handoff"     # shift handoff left ticket unclaimed
    TOOL_FAILURE        = "tool_failure"        # alert system itself failed
    CUSTOMER_HOLD       = "customer_hold"       # pause was not applied when it should have been
    UNKNOWN             = "unknown"

@dataclass
class PostBreachReview:
    ticket_id: str
    priority: str
    breach_state_reached: str           # highest state before resolution
    breach_duration_hours: float        # how long past 100% before resolved
    root_cause: BreachRootCause
    root_cause_detail: str
    corrective_action: str
    reviewed_by: str
    completed_at: datetime

    def requires_credit(self) -> bool:
        # Enterprise P0 breaches and extended P1 breaches trigger automatic credit
        return self.breach_state_reached in ("breached", "extended")

    def credit_percentage(self) -> float:
        match self.priority:
            case "P0":
                # 5% per breach hour, cap at 20%
                return min(0.20, self.breach_duration_hours * 0.05)
            case "P1":
                return 0.02 if self.breach_state_reached == "breached" else 0.05
            case _:
                return 0.0

The corrective_action field is the most important part of the review. A root cause without a corrective action is a description, not a review. The action must be specific and ownable: not "improve alerting" but "add a secondary PagerDuty escalation path for P0 tickets when the primary assignee does not acknowledge the alert within 10 minutes by [owner] by [date]." Reviews without specific corrective actions are returned to the reviewer for revision.

The breach escalation process produces a set of program health metrics that the support manager reviews weekly and the VP of Engineering reviews monthly. The goal is not zero breaches — that would require overprovisioning the support team to handle hypothetical peak loads at all times. The goal is that breaches are rare, handled quickly when they occur, and that each breach produces a corrective action that reduces the probability of the same failure mode recurring.
Repeat breach same root cause (30-day window)

The warning-to-breach conversion rate is the canary metric for the effectiveness of the 80% warning. If warnings are firing but agents are still missing the breach, either the warning is going to the wrong channel, the agent workload is too high to respond even with a warning, or the warning message is not urgent enough to stand out from routine alert noise. A conversion rate above 20% requires immediate investigation of the warning notification path.

AI application support breaches differ from classical SaaS support breaches in one critical way: the nature of the issue is often ambiguous at intake. A customer who reports "the model is not working" may be experiencing a transient API error (P2), a systematic quality degradation (P1), or a safety violation that triggered a model refusal they weren't expecting (immediate L3 escalation regardless of SLA tier). Misclassifying the priority at intake is therefore one of the most common causes of first-response SLA breaches — the ticket was filed at P2, the agent prepared a standard P2 response, and by the time the actual issue severity was understood the P1 SLA had already expired.

These are the hardest to catch because the model continues to respond — it just responds worse than expected. The customer may not file an urgent ticket immediately. By the time they report it, they may have been experiencing degraded output for hours. The breach response must include both the standard notification chain and a check of whether the degradation is systemic (affecting all customers) or isolated (affecting one customer's use case). Systemic degradation elevates the priority by one level.

Failures that only manifest at very long prompts are often not reproducible in L1's testing environment because L1 agents test with shorter inputs. These tickets look like "works for me" from L1's perspective, causing delay in escalation while the customer's issue is real and ongoing. The breach escalation for these tickets should include automatic escalation to L2 if the ticket contains any mention of context length, token count, or input size.

Customers who receive an unexpected refusal from the model file high-urgency tickets because their application is blocked. These are sometimes filed as P0 even though the refusal was intended behavior — it was a safety policy acting correctly. Breach escalation for safety-category tickets must include a fast path to the policy team to confirm whether the refusal was correct. If it was correct, the customer communication changes: the resolution is explanation and workaround design, not a fix.

When a new model version is deployed, regression reports may arrive in a burst. The first ticket of a rollout regression may look like an isolated P2; the second and third tickets on the same symptom within 30 minutes should automatically trigger a P0 rollout review, elevating any open tickets on that symptom to P1 and restarting their SLA clocks from the elevated priority.

When a major incident is active, the breach escalation process must adapt. Normal SLA tracking continues — tickets opened during an incident are still tracked against their SLA windows — but the automatic reassignment and manager notification paths change because the support manager and engineering team are already engaged on the incident. Routing every SLA breach notification to the incident channel during a P0 incident creates noise at the worst possible time.

The incident-aware escalation context prevents the situation where a P0 breach fires a manager notification into the incident channel at exactly the moment the incident commander is in the middle of a decision about a rollback. The breach is still tracked and reviewed post-incident, but during the incident the notification path adapts to the existing communication structure. After the incident is resolved and the cooling period ends, normal breach escalation resumes for any tickets that are still open.
Integrating Breach Escalation with Customer Success

For enterprise customers, an SLA breach is not just a support event — it is an account health event. The account executive and technical account manager need to know about breaches as they happen (not after the fact in a monthly report) so they can manage the customer relationship proactively. A customer who learns about their SLA breach from their own monitoring before the AE reaches out is a customer who is already building a case for contract renewal leverage or churn.

The recommended_message field is drafted by the support manager based on the breach details and root cause review. The AE personalises it before sending — they should not send the draft verbatim, because it reads as generic. The goal is to ensure the AE has the substance of the message correct (the credit amount, the corrective action, the apology tone) while allowing them to personalise it for the specific customer relationship. A generic apology from a company is far less effective than a personalised one from the person who manages the account.

SLA breach escalation is a multi-stage process: a warning fires at 80% of the SLA window, a hard alert and auto-reassignment at 100%, and a management-level escalation at 150%. Customer communication templates and maximum send delays ensure the customer is informed promptly. Every qualifying breach triggers a post-breach review with a specific corrective action. Program health is tracked through five metrics, with the warning-to-breach conversion rate serving as the leading indicator for the effectiveness of the warning stage.
